ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore Chilli Crab. FOODON_03302757 (crab dish) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crabs as a category; no specific chilli crab preparation code exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore Chilli Crab or a sufficiently close parent dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A medium serving of Singapore Chilli Crab typically consists of one whole mud crab (approximately 600 g including shell and sauce), shared between two diners, often accompanied by mantou (fried buns) for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central Crab cooked moist heat (adapted with sauce components)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Chilli Crab as a Singaporean dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for chilli crab (per 100 g edible portion including sauce), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked crab (NDB 15144) for core macros. The sauce components (tomato, chilli, egg, sugar) contribute additional carbohydrates and sodium. Values represent the mixed edible portion (crab meat plus sauce) at approximately 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Seafood Dishes — analogous estimate based on shellfish and tomato-based sauce components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chilli Crab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous profile of cooked shellfish (GI ~0 for protein-dominant component) combined with a tomato-chilli sauce containing sugar and cornstarch. The sauce carbohydrates are partially offset by protein and fat, yielding a blended low GI estimate consistent with similar mixed seafood-sauce dishes in the Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 34.8) / 100 ≈ 12.2, rounded to 12.5 for a 600 g serving at 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g (edible portion ~60 g net carbohydrate-contributing sauce, approximately 34.8 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce, fermented shrimp paste, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to a small serving (150–200 g edible portion) and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible, as a full medium crab serving can contribute 700–900 mg sodium, representing 30–40% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from tomato-chilli sauce containing sugar and cornstarch, with low GI (~35) mitigated by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Chilli Crab in moderate portions (150–200 g edible crab and sauce) as the low GI (~35) and high protein content help moderate postprandial glucose response, but should avoid accompanying fried mantou buns which significantly increase the glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g) and sodium content, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from crab meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Chilli Crab occasionally and in moderate portions, noting that crab provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, but the high sodium content and cooking oil used in the sauce preparation warrant caution for those managing blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chilli Crab (辣椒螃蟹, Ketam Cili) is one of Singapore's most iconic national dishes and a cornerstone of Singaporean culinary identity. It consists of whole mud crab (typically Scylla serrata) stir-fried and simmered in a rich, semi-thick sauce made from fresh chillies, tomato paste, garlic, ginger, shallots, fermented shrimp paste (belacan), soy sauce, sugar, egg, and cornstarch. The dish is characterised by its vibrant red-orange sauce that is simultaneously savoury, mildly spicy, slightly sweet, and tangy. The crab meat is tender and succulent, absorbing the complex flavours of the sauce. Chilli Crab is traditionally served with deep-fried or steamed mantou (Chinese buns) used to soak up the sauce. The dish is a staple at seafood restaurants across Singapore, particularly at East Coast Seafood Centre and Jumbo Seafood. Nutritionally, Chilli Crab is a moderate-calorie dish (~105 kcal per 100 g edible portion) rich in high-quality protein (11.5 g per 100 g), providing essential amino acids, zinc (3.5 mg), selenium (36 mcg), and vitamin B12 (3.2 mcg). The sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and belacan, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The dish is not halal due to the use of fermented shrimp paste and potential cross-contamination. A medium crab typically weighs 600–800 g whole and serves 1–2 people.

Chilli Crab vs Black Pepper Crab

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo lists Chilli Crab as a Singaporean dish with nutrient data. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked crab data. The sauce composition introduces variability across restaurants, and the medium-size designation (approximately 600 g whole crab) is restaurant-specific. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples' which is incorrect for a crab dish; corrected to 'Fish and seafood'. 2. The cuisine tag 'Noodle' in the input is not applicable to Chilli Crab; the canonical cuisine is Singaporean Chinese. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source for this dish. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked crab; the sauce components do not contribute measurable trans fat. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (35, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous seafood-sauce dish profile; no direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chilli Crab. 7. The dish is not halal due to fermented shrimp paste (belacan) which may contain non-halal shellfish fermentation products; halal-certified versions exist at some restaurants but are not the standard preparation. 8. Serving size of 600 g represents the whole medium crab including shell; edible yield is approximately 35–40% (210–240 g edible meat and sauce). 9. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy crab data and are approximate. 10. The price of SGD 108 for a medium crab at Bei Jiang Restaurant is consistent with premium seafood restaurant pricing in Singapore.
